observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:

Require all concerned accountable officers to immediately settle all unliquidated cash advances through the submission of necessary supporting documents. Otherwise, withholding of salaries shall be contemplated to enforce collection and or liquidation as provided in the same Circular;
observations.managementAction:
The Management strictly monitored the Cash Advances (CA) granted to Officers and Employees of the LGU.

The Management committed to pursue extra efforts in collecting the unliquidated cash advances.

The Accounting Office implemented the No Liquidation, No CA policy to all municipal employees.
observations.reason:
As of December 31, 2024, three (3) officers and employees who were granted cash advances in CY 2022-2023 have not yet liquidated the cash advances. While the cash advances of other officers and employees have already been liquidated, majority of the balances were reclassified to Due from Officers and Employees.

There was no evidence that the Management pursued collection efforts, as no copies of demand or collection letters were submitted to support the compliance.

While the Management have complied with the rules in the granting of cash advances, some cash advances granted during the CY 2024 remained outstanding at for several months due to the lack of timely liquidation.

observations.recommendation:

Furthermore, it is crucial to undertake all other procedures prescribed under COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, to ensure that the reconciled and accurate balances of PPE accounts are reflected in the financial statements.

observations.reason:
The Management has completed the inventory of PPEs of the LGU; however, there were still unreconciled differences records between accounting records and Report on the Physical Count of PPE.

The Management has yet to submit required reports related to the cleansing of PPEs.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management strictly enforce the COA Orders of Execution (COE) by requiring the Municipal Treasurer to withhold salaries or other compensation due the persons liable in satisfaction of the disallowed transactions in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We also recommended that the Management advise the persons liable to submit a request for installment pursuant to COA Resolution No. 2015-031 dated August 20, 2015, as amended by COA Resolution No. 2017-021 dated November 3, 2017 subject to the approval by the Commission for all settlements to be made through installment.
observations.reason:
Settlement efforts during the year were minimal, and no  collection efforts have been initiated against the other liable individuals. 

No request for installment in connection with the settlement of disallowances has not yet been submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management strictly follow the rules in granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances as prescribed in COA Circular No. 97-002 in order to forestall the accumulation of unliquidated cash advances. Accordingly, the Management should require all concerned employees to immediately settle all unliquidated cash advances through the submission of necessary supporting documents. Otherwise, failure of the employee to liquidate his/her cash advances within the prescribed period shall constitute a valid cause by the Management to withhold of his/her salary or any money due him/her in accordance with paragraphs 9.3.2 and 9.3.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that the Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to correct the errors in recording purchases and issuances of inventories and observe the Perpetual Inventory System as method of recording inventories as prescribed under Section 51, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs.
Direct the Municipal General Services Officer to complete and submit the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) using the prescribed format for the result of inventory of all supplies account to the Office of the Auditor not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively; and
Reconcile balances per accounting records and the result of complete physical count of Inventories.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal Assessor, should furnish the Municipal Accountant with a complete and certified list showing the names of the taxpayers with the amounts due and collectible for the year as basis in setting up the correct Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax Receivables at the beginning of the year in accordance with Section 20, Volume 1 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs. Moreover, the Municipal Accountant should ensure any delinquent accounts be continuously reported in the books unless subsequently settled.

Based on the above observations, we recommended the Management the following:
The Municipal Accountant (i) prepare ledger cards that would completely account all items composing the PPE account in the financial statements; (ii) compute the depreciation expense and accumulated depreciation of all the depreciable assets; and (iii) derecognize unserviceable properties from the books upon disposal.
Require the Inventory Committee to perform a complete physical count covering all classes of properties owned and controlled by the Municipality; reconcile the accounting and inventory records; adjust the PPE accounts in the books as necessary; prepare and submit the corresponding report together with the established subsidiary ledger for each class of PPE.
Complete and submit the Inventory of all Local Roads and Road Map and Local Road Network Property Cards, Report on Local Road Network of the Municipality as shown in Annex A of COA Circular No. 2015-008. The preparation of the Report of Local Road Network must be observed yearly. Also, keep and maintain the Local Road Network Property/Property Ledger Card for all roads and its components.
Instruct the Bids and Awards Committee and the General Services Officer or his equivalent to prepare the necessary documents, including IIRUP, for the immediate disposal of the unserviceable properties in order to forestall further deterioration of the properties and take advantage of the possible income from sale thereof and to be derecognized from the accounting records.

We recommended the Management to require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entry to observe the capitalization threshold pursuant to COA Circular No. 2024-006 dated March 14, 2024 to present fairly the affected accounts in the financial statements.
Also, we recommended the use, maintenance, and regular updating of the related reports prescribed per COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022, for proper distinction and monitoring of Semi-Expendable Inventories.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant to initiate action to reconcile with the Accountant of the Cagayan Valley Center for Health and Development (CVCHD), Department of Health (DOH) Region 02, to correct any discrepancies in the recording of transactions for a fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements of both the DOH and the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant to prepare and record adjusting entries to fairly present the affected accounts in accordance with the accounting and reporting requirements under Republic Act No. 10121 and COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012. A copy of the Journal Entry Voucher should also be submitted immediately to the Office of the Auditor for verification and proper audit documentation.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review all accounts affecting the Government Equity under Trust Fund, prepare the necessary adjusting entries and transfer the identified items as Government Equity to the General Fund books.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant properly record all financial transactions using the appropriate account titles based on the actual description of transactions in conformity with the revised chart of accounts in order to permit Management to review its activities according to areas of responsibilities and allow for a clearer and fair presentation of transactions in the financial statements of the Municipality.
Accordingly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw the Journal Entry Voucher to reclassify all accounts charged to Other Maintenance of Operating Expenses (Other MOOE) account to the most appropriate accounts in accordance with the prescribed Chart of Accounts.

We commended the dedication and commitment of the Local Government of Dinapigue for securing and successfully completing vital development initiatives through the DA-PRDP I-BUILD subproject, which effectively addresses the needs of the community and promotes sustainable development aligned with the objectives of the PRDP. Moving forward, we recommended that the LGU institute an operation and maintenance audit system to monitor the operation and maintenance compliance of the LGU for a period of ten (10) years in accordance with the Phase 6 of the development process of the I-BUILD Manual.

We recommended that the LGU ensure that all procurement be meticulously and judiciously planned to promote the efficient and effective use of government funds, maximizing the benefits for the community.
Additionally, we recommended the LGU through the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to submit the identified lacking documents to support the variation order and strictly observe the documentary and procedural requirements of the Procurement Law to preclude the suspension and/or disallowance thereof.
We recommended that the Municipal Engineer inform the contractor to urgently undertake corrective works to prevent further damage and maintain the durability of the structure.

We recommended that Management strictly enforce the COA Orders of Execution (COE) by requiring the Municipal Treasurer to withhold salaries or other compensation due the persons liable in satisfaction of the disallowed transactions in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all claims and payrolls are supported with complete documentation. Likewise, the Disbursing Officer should ascertain that all payrolls are properly received by concerned bona fide beneficiaries by requiring them to sign on the payrolls.

We recommended that Management, thru the Municipal Accountant, strictly ensure all disbursements are supported with complete and appropriate documentation.  For items intended for distribution, such as t-shirts and uniforms, a signed distribution list specifying the names of the recipients, the quantity received, and their signatures as proof of actual distribution should be prepared and attached as part of the supporting documents. The list must clearly specify the names of the recipients, the quantity received, and their signatures as proof of actual distribution.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer should prepare the reports described in Volume II of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs) and to Item No. IV (A.5 -A.8) & Item No. VI (2) of COA Circular 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014. Moreover, both are encouraged to visit and apply the new accounting and reporting guidelines prescribed under COA Circular 2024-006 dated March 14, 2024, for the preparation of the Semi-Expendable Property Cards and Semi-Expendable Property Ledger Cards.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer exercise due care and diligence in monitoring the completion of the monthly Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms to reflect accurate accountability on all the accountable forms to prevent the loss or misuse of government funds and property.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant provide for Allowance for Impairment in order to present fairly the Account Receivables account in the financial statements at its net realizable value, in compliance with IPSAS 29.
observations.managementAction: Management provided an Allowance for Impairment to present Account Receivables at its net realizable value, in compliance with IPSAS 29.

Maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF for disaster preparedness, prevention, and mitigation, in accordance with the guidelines set out in DILG DMB Joint Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013; and

Strategically and properly plan and include only those priority projects and activities that are deemed necessary, which will support the Philippine Disaster Risk Reduction and Management System.

As a result, the utilization rate of the fund significantly increased in CY 2024.
observations.reason: Fully Implemented. | The Management replaced the acting Officer-in-Charge of the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office during the 4th quarter of CY 2023 to oversee the proper implementation of disaster risk reduction programs, ensure compliance with mandated plans and procedures, and strengthen the operational efficiency of the office. As a result, the utilization rate of the fund significantly increased in CY 2024.

However, due to the time-consuming nature of the process and the coordination required with various government agencies, not all parcels have been successfully transferred as of this date. Efforts are ongoing to complete the transfer of remaining titles to fully protect the Municipality’s interests.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the review its LDRRMP and strictly enforce the proper utilization of the LDRRMF to fully support disaster risk management activities of the LDRRMC. Cause the immediate transfer of the unutilized LDRRMF from the General Fund to the Special Trust Fund in accordance with rules and regulations and ensure the transfer of succeeding years’ unspent LDRRMF balances to the STF.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management strictly monitor and fast track the implementation and completion as per programmed timelines of all the LGU’s programs, projects and activities funded under the 20% Development Fund to immediately serve the intended purpose or use thereof to the constituents, and direct the offices concerned to vigorously pursue the completion of the projects in order to achieve the objectives of the said projects in providing socio – economic benefits to the intended beneficiaries.
observations.managementAction: Most of the projects indicated in their Annual Investment Plan were implemented during the calendar year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management recompense the 20% Development Fund for the amount of ₱450,000.00 used for Team Building activities and henceforth ensure to adhere to the guidelines set under Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-01 dated February 22, 2017 of the DILG and DBM in the utilization of 20% Development Fund.
observations.managementAction: Management had already recompense the amount and in compliance the implementation of the JMC No. 2017-01

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management through the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) submit the identified lacking documentation requirements to support the procurement activities conducted and partial payments made and strictly observe the documentary and procedural requirements of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 and strictly require the concluded Contract of Agreement to be properly notarized.
observations.managementAction: Management observes the documentary requirements of RA 9184.
observations.reason: No supporting documents were submitted yet on the identified lacking documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management strictly enforce direct payment to be made in check to suppliers and creditors for all purchases of goods and services as prescribed under Section 93 of PD 1445. Management should prohibit the practice of procuring goods and services through reimbursement unless in emergency cases or the items or services are petty in nature. Management may also consider establishing a Petty Cash Fund to take charge for each type of expenses using the Imprest Fund System to avoid or limit the incurrence of transactions on reimbursement.
observations.managementAction: Management minimizes on the use of reimbursements. However there are still procurements of supplies that were made through reimbursements instead of direct payment.
observations.reason: Restated in Audit Observation No. 11 in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management strictly follow the rules in granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advance as prescribed in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 in order to forestall the accumulation of unliquidated cash advances. The Municipal Mayor direct the concerned officers and employees to settle their unliquidated cash advances, otherwise withholding of salaries shall be contemplated to enforce collection as provided in the same Circular and/or Notice of Suspension shall be issued for all long outstanding unliquidated cash advances.  
The Municipal Accountant shall issue a certification that the previous cash advance has been liquidated and accounted for in the books before granting additional cash advances.  The no liquidation no cash advance policy must be strictly implemented.
observations.managementAction: Management controlled the granting of cash advances. However, liquidations of accountable officers were not made within the prescribed period. There are still long and outstanding balances of unliquidated cash advances.
observations.reason: Restated in Audit Observation No. 3 in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management the following courses of action: Instruct all department heads and concerned officials that hiring of JO personnel should be attributed to specific projects for a period of not exceeding six months. Maximize personnel holding permanent positions to do regular, administrative and clerical functions. Ensure that JOs are identified to specific projects and Project Title should be clearly indicated in the Individual Project Proposal. Assess whether it is more advantageous for LGU Dinapigue to fill up the vacant positions with permanent plantilla positions sourced from the JO and casual employees, or that of hiring of JO employees. Require the JOs to submit duly filled up daily time records or time sheets as well as accomplishment reports to validate the payments made of their wages for services rendered in order to avoid disallowance thereof.
observations.managementAction: Management still continues to hire JOs without proper documentation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the following: Management particularly the HRMO and Accounting Office strictly comply with the existing rules and regulations stated above in the preparation and approval of the payrolls to avoid sanctions that may be imposed for non-compliance thereof. In the event that the total authorized deductions reduce the net take home pay to less than ₱4,000.00, the authorized deductions for BIR, GSIS, HDMF and PHIC shall enjoy first preference; those deductions for mutual benefits association, thrift banks and savings and loan association shall enjoy second preference and so forth. The other deductions that are not accommodated during the pay period should already be paid directly by the employee concerned to the loaning institution. The HRMO exercise diligence in certifying the monthly net take home pay of employees and provide emergency measures to assuage the situation of those who have been negatively affected by their loan deductions.
observations.managementAction: Management had undertaken the recommendations by monitoring and controlling the payroll of the employees.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Management particularly the Human Resource Office and Accounting Office to strictly comply with the existing rules and regulations stated above in the preparation of payrolls and preparation of remittances to avoid sanctions that may be imposed for non-compliance thereof and to coordinate with Pag-IBIG Office for the over remittance of contributions. Likewise, submit to our respective office the result of consultation with DBM pertaining to the propose increase for Pag-IBIG contributions, if any.
observations.managementAction: Management complied with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management enforce the full settlement of the suspended transactions by immediate submission of the required documents sought for in the Notices of Suspensions. Moreover, the Local Chief Executive should strictly enforce the COE in settlement of the audit disallowances in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006.
observations.managementAction: Management have partially collected settlements on the NDs, however management overlooked the settlements for Notices of Suspensions.
observations.reason: Restated in Audit Observation No. 7 in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation:
The Local Finance Committee make a systematic and realistic forecasting of revenue taking into account prior years’ experience and statistics as aids in setting targets. 

The Municipal Treasurer and other Departments involved in generating revenues endeavor to meet its targets. Intensify all collection efforts. Periodic review should be made by concerned officials to ascertain whether collection targets are attained. The reasons for the shortfall in collections should be analyzed and alternative actions should be looked into in order to realize projections.
observations.managementAction: Management increased the collections as per budgeted on non-tax revenues

observations.recommendation: All unexpended balances of the LDRRMF for 2015 and 2016 should accrue to a Special Trust Fund and be provided with adequate funding or cash pursuant to Section 21, Paragraph 3 of the Republic Act No. 10121. We also recommended the opening of a separate bank account for this Fund.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by management due to unavailability of funds. Supporting documents to ascertain the balances of previous unutilized amount are not available.
observations.reason: Restated in Audit Observation No. 5 in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that claims be processed  and  paid  only  after  it
   passes  the  criteria  of  propriety  and  completeness  of   documentary
   requirements  have  been  checked  to  avoid  audit   disallowances   and
   suspensions.

We recommended that to the Local Chief  Executive  allow  only  the
    designated special disbursing officer at the  sub-office  at  Ilagan  to
    take charge of disbursing petty cash expenses  and  not  allowing  other
    personnel to do reimbursements too.

We recommended that  the  Municipal  Accountant  ensure  that  only
   lawful expenditures and obligations incurred during the year be taken  up
   in the books of accounts of that  year.   Avoid  paying  expenditures  of
   prior year/s that were not obligated in that year of incurrence to avoid
   depletion of funds.

We recommended  that  the  Municipal  Accountant  ensure  that  all
   disbursements for payments of goods and services including contracts  are
   subjected to value-added tax as  required  under  the  Tax  Code  of  the
   Philippines (NIRC). Collect from the  suppliers/dealers  and  contractors
   the corresponding value-added taxes and remit the  same  to  the  BIR  to
   avoid penalties imposed by the regulation.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant only remits  what  has
   been  deducted  in  the  payrolls  to  avoid  over-remittances  of   loan
   repayments. We also recommended the reconstruction of  subsidiary  ledger
   for each employee to determine the amount due from them  and  immediately
   collect, if not, deduct the amount from their salaries.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive adopt strict measures
   to regulate  the  granting  of  cash  advances  in  accordance  with  the
   provisions of COA Circular 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and  adhere  to
   Item 6.0 COA Circular 2012-004.  Any  unused  or  unspent  cash  advances
   should be returned or refunded.

        We further  recommended  that  the  Municipal  Accountant  strictly
   monitor the settlement of  unliquidated  cash  advances  and  discontinue
   granting additional cash advances unless all  outstanding  cash  advances
   have been liquidated.

We recommended that management require the  Accounting  Office  and
   Treasury Office or General Services Office to reconcile their  respective
   records to come up with the correct balance of the  Property,  Plant  and
   Equipment accounts. Require the Accounting Office to provide and  compute
   the depreciation based on Philippine Public  Sector  Accounting  Standard
   for all depreciable assets/properties in order  to  reflect  the  correct
   valuation of the PPE accounts.

We recommended that the Accounting  Office  exert  extra  effort  to
   analyze and determine the details of the accounts and draw the  necessary
   adjustments, if  necessary  and  to  maintain  subsidiary  ledgers  for
   each account.

We recommended that management strictly adhere to the provisions of
   Joint Memorandum Circular of the DILG and  DBM,  that  only  expenditures
   related to development  projects  are  to  be  charged  against  the  20%
   Development Fund.

We recommended that all unexpended balances of the LDRRMF for  2015
   and 2016 should accrue to a Special  Trust  Fund  and  be  provided  with
   adequate funding or cash pursuant  to  Section  21,  Paragaph  3  of  the
   Republic Act No. 10121. We also recommended the  opening  of  a  separate
   bank account for this Fund.

We recommended that strict compliance with the  provisions  of  the
   DBM, NEDA and NCRFW Joint Circular No. 2004-1  dated  April  5,  2004  be
   observed. At least 5% of the total appropriation should be observed and a
   GAD plan should be  formulated  identifying  programs/projects/activities
   that will address the issues and concerns for the  promotion  of  gender-
   responsive  governance,  protection  and  fulfillment  of  women’s  human
   rights, and promotion of women’s economic development.

We recommended that all contract agreements be  submitted  together
   with complete  supporting  documents  within  five  (5)  days  after  its
   perfection to facilitate our audit and to  avoid  audit  suspensions.  We
   request all copies of contract agreements be submitted to the  Office  of
   the Auditor.
